title: Managing mediocrity: Indonesia’s defence-partnership portfolio
author: IISS
text:
Evan A. Laksmana @EvanLaksmanaShangri-La Dialogue Senior Fellow for Southeast Asian Security and Defence, and Editor, Asia-Pacific Regional Security Assessment About Evan A.

Shangri-La Dialogue 2026

Online Analysis4th June 2026

Managing mediocrity: Indonesia’s defence-partnership portfolioIndonesia has signed 87 bilateral defence and security commitments with 36 countries worldwide in the last 25 years. This plethora of non-binding commitments, however, has not had far-reaching effects for Indonesia’s military effectiveness or defence readiness.

Jakarta has recently signed a string of major defence deals. In early February 2026, it signed the Treaty on Common Security with Australia, which, among other things, commits Jakarta and Canberra to ‘consult each other in the case of adverse challenges to either Party or to their common security interests’. In April, Indonesia signed the Major Defence Cooperation Partnership with the United States, anchored around military modernisation, exercises, education and training. Perhaps significantly, via this partnership, both countries will also explore ‘co-developing sophisticated asymmetric capabilities … in the maritime, subsurface, and autonomous systems domains’, as well as on maintenance, repair and overhaul support.
In May, Jakarta and Tokyo signed a Defence Cooperation Arrangement that reportedly covers humanitarian assistance, disaster relief, maritime security, joint exercises and potential exchanges in defence technology and equipment. The deal came just as Japan further relaxed its decades-old restrictions on arms exports. Considering that President Prabowo Subianto also met with his counterparts from Australia, France, Japan, Russia, the United Kingdom and the US from January to May 2026, some are viewing the defence deals as par for the course for Indonesia’s non-aligned hedging posture.
All of this assumes that Jakarta has a fully developed and functioning strategy, which remains uncertain given its disordered strategic-policymaking ecosystem. More importantly, when placed within the longer arc and broader landscape of Indonesia’s defence diplomacy, the three new deals do not fundamentally change the country’s diverse but arguably mediocre defence-partnership portfolio. Ultimately, they do not substantially enhance Indonesia’s military effectiveness and defence readiness, nor do they commit the country to deeper ties across different strategic domains beyond limited areas of security activities.
Paper portfolio
Indonesia has publicly signed 87 bilateral defence and security cooperation commitments with 36 countries between 2001 and 2025. These documents varied in their legal and political obligations, were spearheaded by – and thus further applied to – different agencies and covered a wide range of issues (see Figure 1). While this defence and security commitment portfolio seems large, it remains underwhelming in strategic value. For one thing, most of the documents were memorandums of understanding or of cooperation, joint statements, letters of intent or administrative arrangements, with very few legally binding commitments, if any. Almost none of them commit Indonesia to far-reaching security commitments with any single power; rather, most of them start with phrases underscoring ‘sovereignty’, ‘independence’, ‘territorial integrity’, ‘equality’ and ‘mutual benefit’.  

Indonesia’s deeply entrenched instinct towards non-alignment underpins such a normative posture. The disadvantage of this is that the commitments will not directly or automatically translate into advanced defence platforms, broad operational access, deep industrial collaboration, joint contingency planning or top-level intelligence sharing. Most of the documents are agency-specific direction-of-travel guidelines, rather than inter-agency strategic commitments. That few of the documents cover specific areas salient for capability development, like defence industry, defence technology or cyber security (see Figure 1), exacerbates the challenge.
Furthermore, the states that Indonesia signed deals with are incredibly diverse in terms of strategic profile and importance, ranging from traditional defence partners like Australia and the US, to emerging ones like South Korea and Türkiye, to far-flung states like Azerbaijan, Ecuador, Greece, Guinea-Bissau and Mozambique. Around a dozen states, including Australia, China, France, Japan, Russia, South Korea, Türkiye and the US, are the core providers of Indonesia’s arms and equipment as well as education, training and exercise needs. Indonesia has signed more defence and security commitments with Australia (at least 10) than any other country; China comes a close second with 8 documents, while Russia is at seven, the US is at six and Japan at five.
But outside those core dozen states, there is less strategic coherence and defence value for Indonesia. Documents signed with many of the far-flung countries appear to be generic partnership-commitment templates. The quantity of defence partners, therefore, does not equate to quality, nor can it replace the potential depth of defence relationships. That Australia had to sign almost a dozen commitments before the 2026 treaty indicates that many building blocks are required for a deep partnership.
Patchy landscape
Signed documents are only one component of a defence partnership, albeit an important one that sets basic parameters. The broader landscape of defence partnerships covers a wide range of activities, from institutionalised dialogue mechanisms, arms and equipment procurement, combined-military exercises, education and training exchanges, to defence-industrial collaboration and joint contingency planning or intelligence sharing. Indonesia’s ‘splitting the spoils’ approach to bilateral defence diplomacy results in different partners providing these different components of defence.
The US and Australia, for example, have been the top providers of exercises, education and training for Indonesian officers for decades. More than 7,000 Indonesian students studied at over 200 US professional military education programmes from 2002 to 2018. Meanwhile, France, Italy, Russia, South Korea, Türkiye, the UK and the US have been among the top providers of weapons systems to Indonesia for over a decade now. Indonesia also had 22 separate defence-offset programmes with 16 countries a decade ago, including Brazil, Canada, France, Germany and Russia. France also has a longer history of defence-industrial collaboration with Indonesia, going back decades.
In short, there is no single top provider for all of Indonesia’s defence needs. Different partners provide different goods, which aligns with Indonesia’s ‘retail approach’ to defence planning, which consists of several countries providing limited sets of different weapons systems, enough to operate in some areas but not to fully or sustainably cover Indonesia’s expansive threat vectors. Such an approach has likely created path-dependent inter-operability challenges, both at the platform level (between different systems supplied by different countries) and at the organisational level, between the education, training and exercises provided by different countries. Taken together, it is unclear how a hodgepodge collection of different defence partners providing different goods could lead to a properly functioning joint force with organisational coherence and inter-operable equipment, compatible and usable doctrines, and a sustainable maintenance, repair and overhaul supply chain.
Arrested development
While defence partnerships could be measured by their contribution towards a country’s foreign or domestic policy goals, the ultimate yardstick should still be the extent to which they add value to its defence-capability development. Indeed, Indonesia’s Ministry of Defense has legally formalised the mantra that ‘diplomacy is the first line of defence’. Compared to other countries, Indonesia has certainly been more willing to engage its counterparts from all over the world for the past quarter of a century. Its geostrategic location between the Indian and Pacific oceans, its non-aligned geopolitical instincts and its tremendous geo-economic resources have impelled many countries to develop defence partnerships with it.
But without a mature strategic-thought process and a cohesive policy framework, Indonesia often reacts to – rather than proactively shapes – defence-partnership offers. This is partly why Indonesia has ended up with a portfolio of mediocre defence partnerships – valuable enough to meet certain specific needs in a siloed manner, politically broad enough to keep the partners engaged without formal commitments, but not forceful enough to fundamentally enhance its overall defence capability and readiness.
The plethora of written defence and security commitments with an overly diverse set of countries, after all, dilutes any cumulative strategic benefits in terms of capability development. The Indonesian military operates an array of haphazard, overly diversified weapons systems from dozens of countries that officials privately admit do not really work well together. Despite sending hundreds of officers overseas annually, they continue to be deployed for domestic socio-political-economic tasks upon returning. At a time of immense geopolitical uncertainty, the more worrying strategic profile for Indonesia may be the strategic bandwidth it spends on its mediocre defence-partnerships portfolio.
